title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Emily Bustard suggests to Hercule Poirot, Colonel Weston, and Inspector Cochran that Eleanor Marshall’s murder was likely committed by a former lover, arguing that the victim's character is the primary clue. Poirot agrees, noting that Eleanor herself is the best clue to her own murder. While Inspector Cochran suspects Bustard due to her apparent grudge, Poirot observes that she had been under his supervision until she went to sea with Mr. Redfern.

Rosamond Dane, the owner of the fashion company Rosamond's, provides a statement to the investigators. An old acquaintance of the Marshall family, she admits she disliked Eleanor due to her intolerant nature. Rosamond states she was at Sunlight Cliff from 10:30 until 11:40 that morning reading and watching the sea, but she did not see Eleanor or any boats. When questioned about blackmail or affairs, Rosamond expresses surprise at the idea of blackmail but suggests that Kenneth Marshall might be too foolish to realize the extent of his wife's behavior. She concludes that the killer is likely a man because Eleanor was strangled.

During a search of Eleanor’s bedroom, Inspector Clegg and Colonel Weston discover a bundle of letters. Among Eleanor's extensive collection of clothing and hats, the men find a letter from "J.N." to Eleanor. In the correspondence, J.N. expresses an intense, mad love for her and mentions his departure for China. He thanks her for a check that prevented his prosecution and expresses a desire to buy her emeralds. Inspector Clegg suggests investigating whether J.N. actually went to China, noting that his obsession with Eleanor could provide a motive for the murder.
